rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE MARILYN ANN MALLEN TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Reconsideration (Added For The Purposes Of Posting Tr).
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 3) Joinder of Daniel Mallen The failed and "curable" joinder of Mr. Mallen is not a new fact or circumstance. The failure of Mr. Mallen to join the Petition happened on January 30, 2024, the day of the original hearing on the Petition. As a result, Ms. Calhoun had reason to know of the failed joinder over three (3) months prior to the June 11 hearing. The Court raised the potential standing issue at the January 30 hearing, and yet, Ms. Calhoun did not ensure the sufficiency of the joinder prior to the June 11 hearing. Ms. Calhoun's argument that Judge Castellanos lacked legal authority for her statement that Mr. Mallen's joinder could not "cure" the Petition may be a basis for appeal of the prior order but is not a proper basis for reconsideration. Mr. Mallen's joinder was simply not before the Court at that time. Nothing in Judge Castellanos's Order prevented Mr. Mallen from filing his own Petition for Interim Trust Orders. Even if Ms. Calhoun had been reasonably unaware of Mr. Mallen's failed joinder, Mr. Mallen never did cure his joinder, and he currently takes no position on this Motion for Reconsideration. (Daniel Mallen Estate Matter Status Report at 1:8-11.) It remains the case that Mr. Mallen has not joined Ms. Calhoun's Petition for Interim Orders, and the factual circumstances underlying Ms. Calhoun's lack of standing remain the same. Thus, Mr. Mallen's "surprise" failed joinder does not warrant reconsideration. Nor does it warrant the Court's sua sponte reconsideration of the prior order. Equitable Principles To the extent that Ms. Calhoun cites general equitable principles that were not raised in her Petition for Interim Orders, she provides no explanation of diligence as to why those arguments and authorities were not presented in her Petition. Such legal principles were in existence and knowable to Ms. Calhoun at the time that she filed the Petition. Thus, equitable principles do not provide a basis for reconsideration. As a result of the foregoing, the Motion for Reconsideration is DENIED. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE MARILYN ANN MALLEN TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Reconsideration (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tr)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 3) A party moving for reconsideration must "show diligence with a satisfactory explanation for not having presented the new or different information earlier." (Even Zohar Constr. & Remodeling, Inc. v. Bellaire Townhouses, LLC (2015) 61 Cal.4th 830, 839.) The party may not simply resubmit facts, circumstances, or law previously raised in the first motion and call them "new or different" for the purposes of Code of Civil Procedure section 1008. (See Kerns v. CSE Ins. Grp. (2003) 106 Cal. App. 4th 368, 383.) Ordinarily, a motion for reconsideration should be made to the judge who issued the prior order. However, if the first judge is unavailable, a judge in the same court may hear the motion for reconsideration. (Williamson v. Mazda (2012) 212 Cal.App.4th 449, 454-55; Ziller Electronics v. Sup. Ct. (1988) 206 Cal.App.3d 1222, 1232.) Here, the Notice of Entry of Order for the prior order was served by mail to a California address on July 9, 2024. Because the Notice was served by mail within the state of California, the deadline for filing a motion for reconsideration was extended by five (5) calendar days. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1013, subd. (a).) As a result, the Motion for Reconsideration is timely, as it was filed on July 24, 2024, fifteen (15) days after the Notice of Entry of Order was filed and served by mail. While the Prior Order was issued by Judge Castellanos, the motion is nonetheless proper as Judge Castellanos is a visiting judge who would need to be recalled to hear the instant motion. Even though the motion is timely and properly before this Court, it presents no new facts, circumstances, or law warranting reconsideration. Instead, it merely resubmits circumstances in existence and known to both the Court and Petitioner Calhoun at the time the prior order was issued. Developments in Estate Matter The settlement in the estate matter and thus-far non-disclosure of the settlement terms are not new facts or circumstances unknowable to Petitioner prior to the June 11 hearing on the Petition. Both Petitioner and the Court had actual knowledge of these circumstances as evidenced by the June 11 hearing's mini-minutes showing that discussions were held regarding the settlement agreement. Even if the estate settlement did present a new fact or circumstance warranting reconsideration, it does not change the Court's prior finding that Petitioner lacks the beneficiary standing required to seek orders regarding the internal affairs of the trust. Distribution of Trust Assets A circumstance wherein the trust assets would be distributed and leave funds insufficient to cover probate costs is not a new circumstance as this is precisely why Ms. Calhoun filed her Petition in the first place: "[T]he co-Trustees initially indicated that they would hold a reserve of at least $300,000 for such expenses associated with the Administration of Mrs. Mallen's Estate. By mid-November, however, the co-Trustees indicated a contrary intent." (Petition for Interim Orders 13:3-6.) Petitioner may not simply resubmit facts, circumstances, or law previously raised in the first motion and call them "new or different." (See Kerns v. CSE Ins. Grp., supra,at 383.) Additionally, the co-trustee's request to distribute trust assets was known to Judge Castellanos when she signed the Order denying the Petition.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE MARILYN ANN MALLEN TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Re: Emilie Calhoun?S Petition For Orders: 1) To Recover Some Of The Funds Distributed From Trust; 2) Compelling Co-Trustees To Account And Report; 3) Construing Certain Provisions Of Trust Instrument; 4) Instructing Trustees To Deposit Trust Funds In A Blocked Account, Until Such Time As The Expenses Associated With Administration Of Decedent?S Estate Are Determined [Prob. 1000 And 1008]
rulings.Rulings: (Part 1 of 3) This tentative is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the Court is Petitioner Emilie Calhoun's Motion for Reconsideration of Order Denying with Prejudice Ms. Calhoun's Petition for Orders: (1) To Recover Some of the Funds Distributed from the Trust; (2) Compelling Co-Trustees to Account and Report; (3) Construing Certain Provisions of the Trust Instrument; (4) Instructing Trustees to Deposit Trust Funds in Blocked Account Until Such Time as the Expenses Associated with Administration of Decedent's Estate Are Determined. Upon reconsideration, Ms. Calhoun requests that the Court grant her protective request for relief of a blocked account and her request compelling the co-trustees to account. Alternatively, she asks the Court to either (1) vacate Judge Castellanos's Order Denying the Petition for Interim Trust Orders and reschedule the Petition for a status hearing; or (2) vacate the Order and take the Petition off calendar. To the extent the Motion for Reconsideration is in any way defective, Petitioner requests that the Court use its inherent authority to reconsider the prior order. For the reasons stated below, the Motion for Reconsideration is DENIED. Marilyn Ann Mallen died intestate in 2017. Upon her death, one of Marilyn's nine children, Daniel Mallen, filed a petition for probate of her estate and was later appointed as administrator. With Mr. Mallen initially represented by Ms. Calhoun, he and his siblings engaged in several years of litigation regarding the administration of his mother's estate and the Marilyn Ann Mallen Revocable Trust Dated December 1, 2016. A notice of settlement was filed in the estate matter on March 18, 2024. On April 11, 2023, Ms. Calhoun withdrew as Mr. Mallen's counsel in both the trust and estate matters. Prior to her withdrawal, Mr. Mallen assigned to Ms. Calhoun "the full amount of any unpaid Attorney's fees and costs owing to Attorney as of the date that Attorney ceases to represent client . . . from Client's distributive shares of the Marilyn Mallen Trust and Estate." (Notice of Lien Filed Feb. 22, 2023, at ¶ 3.) Ms. Calhoun filed a Petition for Interim Trust Orders on August 30, 2023, and a hearing was set for October 31, 2023. At that time, the trial in the estate matter was set for November 13, 2023. The Court twice continued the hearing on Ms. Calhoun's Petition for Interim Trust Orders. The Petition eventually came on for hearing before the Honorable Judge Castellanos on June 11, 2024. After taking the matter under submission, the Court denied the Petition, finding that Ms. Calhoun lacked standing because section 6.2 of the Trust instrument prohibits assignment of beneficiary interest. (Order Denying Petition for Interim Orders ["the prior order"].) On July 24, Ms. Calhoun filed the instant Motion for Reconsideration of the June 11 Order. Underlying both the Petition and instant motion is Ms. Calhoun's expectation that the estate assets in the related matter will be insufficient to cover estate-administration expenses, and that therefore, those costs (including the attorney's fees owed to her by Daniel Mallen) will need to be covered pursuant to a provision in the trust instrument providing for payments of outstanding probate debts. (Calhoun Decl. ¶ 6.) Any party affected by an order, may make a motion for reconsideration asking the same court or judge that made the original order to modify, amend, or revoke the prior order based on new or different facts, circumstances, or law. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1008, subd. (a).) The motion must state what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown and must be made within ten (10) days after the party has been served with written notice of the entry of order. (Ibid.) Service by mail extends this period by five (5) calendar days. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 1013, subd. (a).)
